name: CategoryRules
description: Category-specific rules and patterns for Harmony Design System components

categoryClassification.description: Criteria for assigning components to categories

form.description: Components that handle user input and form data
criteria: Accepts user input via interaction (typing, clicking, selecting)
Has a value that can be read/written
Participates in form validation (error, required, disabled states)
Can be disabled, required, or have validation states
Typically used within <form> elements
Manages form field state or groups form fields
form.note: CheckboxGroup is 'form' because it manages validation state for multiple checkboxes, not just layout

display.description: Components that present information or content
criteria: Primary purpose is to display content or information
Does not accept direct user input (read-only or presentational)
May be interactive (clickable, expandable) but doesn't submit data
Focuses on content presentation and visual hierarchy
Primary purpose is navigation or wayfinding
Helps users move between pages, sections, or views
Provides site/app structure awareness
Includes shell/layout navigation elements
navigation.note: Shell components are in navigation category despite providing layout structure

criteria: Communicates system state or user action results
Provides alerts, notifications, or confirmations
Typically temporary or dismissible
Draws user attention to important information

layout.description: Components that arrange other components structurally
criteria: Primary purpose is arranging other components spatially
Controls spacing, positioning, or flow of children
Does not handle data or state itself
Provides structural scaffolding without semantic meaning
No form validation or data management
layout.counterExample: CheckboxGroup is NOT layout because it manages form validation state, despite arranging checkboxes
layout.note: Very few components should be pure layout - most have semantic meaning (form, navigation, etc.)

criteria: Specialized UI for selecting dates, times, or date ranges
Often used as popups or overlays
Provide calendar or time selection interfaces
pickers.note: Could be merged into 'form' category in future - keeping separate for now due to specialized nature

decisionMatrix.description: How to decide between ambiguous categories

scenarios.question: Component groups form inputs AND validates?
scenarios.answer: Category: form (validation is the key differentiator)

scenarios.question: Component accepts input but is presentational?
scenarios.answer: Category: display (if read-only) or form (if writable)

form.description: Patterns for form components and form field composition

fieldComposition.description: Consistent pattern for all form input components

structure.notes: Use semantic <label> element, associate with input via 'for' attribute
The actual form control (input, select, textarea, etc.)
Guidance text, shown when no error present
Error feedback with icon, shown instead of helper text
Visual indicator (search icon, calendar icon, etc.)

fieldComposition.example: Input component: Label (optional) + input wrapper (Icon + input + error message)

groupComposition.description: Pattern for grouping related form controls

structure.notes: Validation message for the entire group

validation.description: When and how to validate form inputs

strategies.use: Most common, balances UX and feedback
strategies.implementation: Validate when user finishes editing a field

strategies.use: Password strength, username availability, format validation

strategies.implementation: Always validate on submit, even with inline validation

errorDisplay.icon: Use Icon component with 'exclamation-circle' or similar
errorDisplay.aria: aria-describedby linking input to error message

requiredFields.position: After label text or inline with label
requiredFields.note: Document whether required fields get asterisk or optional fields get '(optional)'

formLayout.description: How to arrange form fields on a page

patterns.responsive: Stack fields vertically on all breakpoints

patterns.use: Related fields (first name | last name, city | state | zip)
patterns.caution: Only for tightly related fields, can hurt mobile UX
patterns.responsive: Stack to single column on mobile (<768px)

patterns.implementation: Use fieldset or visual sections with headings
patterns.spacing: Larger gap between sections (var(--space-8) or more)

requiredStates.aria: Visible focus indicator required for accessibility

requiredStates.visual: Red border, error icon, error message

requiredStates.note: Different from disabled - value is selectable/copyable

bestPractices.practice: Field width should match expected input length
bestPractices.rationale: Helps users understand how much to type

examples.correct: 2-character state code input is narrow, address input is wide
examples.incorrect: All inputs the same width regardless of content

bestPractices.rationale: Users need to know what to enter
bestPractices.note: Placeholder is not a replacement for label

bestPractices.practice: Tab order should follow visual layout top-to-bottom, left-to-right

display.description: Patterns for presentational and content display components

contentHierarchy.description: How display components establish visual hierarchy
principles: Use typography tokens (display, heading, body) for text hierarchy
Use spacing tokens consistently (tighter spacing for related items)
Use color semantically (success, error, warning, info)
Use elevation for layering (cards, overlays, modals)

interactiveDisplay.description: Display components that respond to interaction

examples.note: Interactive but doesn't submit data - still 'display'

interactiveDisplay.rule: Interactive display components don't manage form data or validation

statusIndicators.description: Components that show status or state
guidelines: Use semantic colors for status (success, error, warning, info)
Provide aria-label for screen readers when status isn't obvious
Badge/Chip should be dismissible if temporary
Loading states (Spinner, ProgressBar) should have accessible loading message

layout.description: Patterns for layout and arrangement components
layout.philosophy: Layout components should be presentational containers that don't manage state or data

spacingResponsibility.description: Who controls spacing: layout parent or content children?
spacingResponsibility.rule: Layout components control spacing between children; content components have no external margins
spacingResponsibility.rationale: Prevents margin collapse issues and makes spacing predictable

implementation.example: ButtonGroup uses CSS gap for spacing between buttons
Button has no external margin - spacing controlled by parent

exceptions.case: Component always needs margin in all contexts
exceptions.solution: Document as exception in component rules
exceptions.example: Section headings might always need top margin

compositionPatterns.description: How to compose layout components with content

patterns.description: Vertical or horizontal arrangement with consistent spacing
Two-dimensional layout with rows and columns
patterns.use: Card grids, data tables, image galleries

patterns.implementation: Stepper + Step components with positional awareness

whenToCreateLayout.description: When to create a layout component vs using CSS
createLayoutComponent: Spacing/arrangement has specific rules (not just basic flexbox/grid)
Responsive behavior is non-trivial (different layouts per breakpoint)
Need named slots for specific positioning
useCompositionOrCSS: Simple flex or grid with default spacing

examples.layoutComponent: ButtonGroup - enforces consistent button spacing, used repeatedly
examples.cssComposition: One-time hero section with image + text - just use CSS Grid

slotBasedComposition.description: Using named slots in layout components
slotBasedComposition.when: Layout has specific regions with different purposes

examples.benefit: Consumers can place content in specific regions

slotBasedComposition.rule: Use slots for fixed-position regions, use default slot for flexible content

bestPractices.practice: Layout components should not manage data or application state
bestPractices.rationale: Keeps layout components reusable and focused
bestPractices.exception: UI state like 'collapsed' for collapsible sidebar is acceptable

bestPractices.practice: Layout components should handle their own responsive behavior
bestPractices.implementation: Use CSS media queries within layout component
bestPractices.example: ButtonGroup stacks vertically on mobile automatically

bestPractices.practice: Use semantic HTML in layout components when applicable

navigation.description: Patterns for navigation and wayfinding components

shellComposition.description: How shell navigation components work together
shellComposition.note: ShellLayout components are categorized as 'navigation' despite providing layout structure

feedback.description: Patterns for feedback and notification components

types.use: Modal confirmations, forms, or critical information

atomicDesignMapping.description: How Harmony components map to Atomic Design methodology
atomicDesignMapping.note: Harmony uses functional categories (form, display, navigation) rather than atomic terminology, but the hierarchy still applies

atoms.description: Smallest, indivisible UI elements - cannot be broken down further

molecules.description: Simple combinations of atoms that function together as a unit
molecules.note: Input is a molecule because it composes Label + Icon + input element

organisms.description: Complex components composed of molecules and/or atoms
organisms.note: DateInput is an organism because it composes Input + DatePicker + PickerPopup + Label + Icon

templates.description: Page-level layouts that compose organisms, molecules, and atoms into structure
templates.note: All Shell* components are templates - they define application structure

benefits.description: Why atomic thinking helps even though we use functional categories
reasons: Helps determine component complexity and scope
Guides composition decisions (atoms compose into molecules, molecules into organisms)
Informs dependency management (molecules depend on atoms, organisms depend on molecules)
Helps with testing strategy (atoms tested in isolation, organisms tested for integration)
Guides documentation depth (atoms need less documentation, organisms need more)

relationshipToCategories.description: Atomic levels and functional categories are orthogonal
relationshipToCategories.explanation: A component can be both an atom AND a form component (e.g., Checkbox), or an organism AND a display component (e.g., Card)
